California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 29,
1992
Jack White
City Attorney
City of Anaheim
200 South Anaheim Boulevard
Suite 356
Anaheim,
CA
92805
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—218
Dear Mr. White:
This •is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of public officials of the City of Anaheim,
including city
counciliuembers, the city manager,
and other employees of the city
regarding their responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Since you
have asked general questions regarding these local public
officials,
and you have not named a specific elected official on
whose behalf you have requested this advice,
we provide the
following informal responses to your questions.
2
Please be aware that advice letters are based on the
particular facts and circumstances of each situation.
Thus, these
responses to your hypothetical questions may not be applicable to
a particular factual situation.
QUESTIONS
1.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by the Walt Disney Company
(“Disney”) to third
parties at the suggestion or recommendation of members of the
Anaheim City Council deemed to be in-kind campaign contributions
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et sec.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page
2
or gifts by Disney to those city councilmembers for purposes of
the Act?
2.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by Disney to third parties at the suggestion or
recommendation of the Anaheim City Manager or his or her clerical
staff deemed gifts by Disney to the Anaheim City Manager for
purposes of the Act?
3.
Does the answer to number
2 above depend upon whether the
Anaheim City Manager has personal knowledge of, or screens,
such
suggestions or recommendations?
4.
Is the answer to number
2 above different if the public
official or employee of the City of Anaheim,
who is the recipient
of the complimentary tickets,
is subject to the disclosure and
disqualification requirements of the Act?
5.
If the answers to numbers
1 and
2 above are in the
affirmative and the Commission treats the complimentary admission
tickets to Disneyland as gifts to the requesting or recommending
public officials,
may the affected Anaheim public officials be
permitted to cure the problem of potential disqualification and of
violation of the gift limits by reimbursing Disneyland, if that is
their desire,
and have such repayments fall within the thirty-day
reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1?
CONCLUSIONS
1 and 2.
Under the facts provided,
we conclude that
complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland which are provided
by the Walt Disney Company to third parties such as family
members,
friends and constituents at the suggestion,
recommendation, or request of members of the Anaheim City Council
or the Anaheim City Manager are deemed to be gifts from the Walt
Disney Company to those city officials for purposes of the gift
limits as well as for the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act.
However,
a “protocol exception” exists where free tickets to
Disneyland are provided to individuals such as other public
officials or visiting dignitaries, or to groups such as schools or
charitable organizations, at the recommendation or request of
members of the Anaheim City Council or Anaheim City Manager acting
in their official capacity.
In that situation, the tickets will
not be treated as gifts to the city councilmembers or the city
manager.
Please note that the “protocol exception” will not apply to
Anaheim city officials or their agents who gain actual physical
possession or have discretion and control over the distribution of
the complimentary tickets to Disneyland.
In such a case, the free
tickets provided by Anaheim city officials to other public
officials and visiting dignitaries, for example, will also be


Our File No.
1—92—218
Page
3
treated as gifts to those city officials.
(See Urch Advice
Letter,
No.
A-91—391 referenced in footnote 6.)
3.
The answer is no.
Presumably the clerical staff are
merely carrying out their job duties at the direction,
and under
the control of the city manager.
As such, the clerical staff are
agents of the city manager.
Thus, whether the city manager
screens, or has personal knowledge of, the recommendations made by
his or her clerical staff does not alter our conclusion set forth
above in response to Questions
1 and 2.
4.
The answer is yes.
It would make a difference if the
city manager’s office is acting as an intermediary for a gift when
the city manager or a member of his clerical staff transmits
requests or recommendations for complimentary tickets to
Disneyland for other city personnel
these other city personnel
are public officials subject to the disclosure and,
disqualification provisions of the Act.
5.
Because of possible confusion concerning whether tickets
left for third parties
(excluding tickets for family members and
friends) at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window at the recommendation
of Anaheim public officials are gifts to those city officials,
we
will permit those city officials to return the tickets or
reimburse Disney for the value of the tickets within 30 days of
issuance of this letter (for tickets received or distributed by
Anaheim public officials within 12 months preceding the date of
this letter).
If that action is taken,
we will not consider the
tickets to be gifts to the officials.
On the other hand, gifts of
tickets which have been received in the past and which are not
returned or reimbursed to Disney following issuance of this letter
may trigger an official’s disqualification.
For purposes of
disqualification, the same applies, of course, to gifts of tickets
worth $250 or more which are received and accepted in the future.
FACTS
One of the business entities owned by the Walt’ Disney Company
is Disneyland,
a leading California tourist attraction which is
visited by millions of guests each year.
It is your understanding
that the Walt Disney Company provides over 300,000 free admission
tickets each year to a wide variety of individuals, nonprofit
organizations,
media groups,
and charities.
Occasionally, public officials,
including Anaheim City
Councilmembers, the Anaheim City Manager, the Governor’s office,
and other state and federal officers, either request that Walt
Disney Company provide them with complimentary tickets for their
own use or the use of their friends, or recommend that Walt Disney
Company provide third parties with complimentary tickets to
Disneyland.
In your letter you indicate that these third parties
include charities, VIPs,
community groups, constituents,
and other
individuals.


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page 4
Apparently public officials are not the only persons who
request complimentary tickets for themselves and their- friends or
who recommend that complimentary tickets be provided to third
parties.
You also state in your letter that other persons or
categories of persons who request complimentary tickets for
themselves or for third parties include media executives, media
personalities, entertainment executives, entertainment
personalities, charitable organizations,
community organizations,
sports teams,
and school leaders.
You inform us that the Walt Disney Company itself determines
whether it will honor, modify, or reject the recommendation of a
public official or other requestor for complimentary tickets for
third parties.
If Disney elects to honor an official’s
recommendation and provide free admission tickets to the
recommended group or individual, it leaves the tickets at its
“Will Call” window in the name of that group or individual.
The
recommending party does not determine whether the free tickets
will be issued to the third party,
does not handle the tickets,
and could not themselves pick up the tickets at the “Will Call”
window since the tickets are held in the name of the third party
recipient.
The Anaheim City Manager and his staff also make
recommendations to the Walt Disney Company that it provide
complimentary admission tickets to third parties.
These third
parties include residents and businesses within the City of
Anaheim, residents and businesses in other communities,
and public
officials from other communities.
In addition, as a convenience to the Walt Disney Company, the
clerical staff in the Anaheim City Manager’s office transmits
requests or recommendations for complimentary tickets for all the
nonelected public officials and employees of the City of Anaheim.
You state that the Anaheim City Manager does not personally review
or approve such requests except in unusual circumstances.
Many of
the city’s nonelected public officials and employees who request
complimentary tickets through the Anaheim City Manager’s office
are also subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Act.
Disney currently records such requests
as coming from the Anaheim City Manager and not from the recipient
or recommending Anaheim public officials or employees.
After the end of each calendar year, Disneyland provides to
the Anaheim City Councilmembers and to the Anaheim City-Manager a
statement reflecting the value of each Disneyland special event
attended by such public officials and the value of all regular
complimentary admission tickets provided to such officials for
their personal or discretionary use.
Disneyland sends this
information to assist the city’s public officials in preparing
“Schedule F” of their respective annual Statements of Economic
Interests.
Recently, Disneyland additionally began including on
this statement the number of complimentary tickets that were left
for third parties at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window in response


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page
5
to recommendations received from the public official or the public
official’s office.
ANALYSI S
Pursuant to the Act,
every public official must disclose all
his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected
by the exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200—87313.)
A “public official” is defined broadly to include
every natural person who is a member, officer,
employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.)
Section 87207 provides that a public official
must disclose the name and address of each source of gifts of $50
or more in value, the amount and the date on which the gift was
received,
and a general description of the business activity of
the donor.
Moreover, Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making or in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial
interest.
(Section 87100.)
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on any donor of,
or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
$250 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).)
And finally, the Act now imposes gift and honoraria limits
with respect to local elected officers.
Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal,
or like
gathering, or any gifts,
from any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
Section 82028(a) defines a “gift” as:
[A]ny payment to the extent that consideration
of eaual or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page
6
Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal
action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by
reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
of proving that the consideration received is of
equal or greater value.
(Emphasis added.)
You have asked a series of general questions concerning the
characterization of free admission tickets to Disneyland,
and if
the tickets are deemed to be gifts, the identity of the donee
under various circumstances.
At the outset of our analysis,
we
would like to clarify two significant points.
First, your letter distinguishes between elected and
nonelected public officials.
The Act makes no such distinction
for financial disclosure or disqualification purposes.
The
definition of “public official” includes elected and nonelected
officials.
The term public official includes every member,
officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency.
(Section 82048.)
Thus, the issue of whether or not the
Disneyland tickets discussed herein are gifts does not turn on the
elected or nonelected status of the concerned public officials.
Second, all the questions addressed in your letter concern
application of the Act’s disclosure and disqualification
provisions.
If,
however, the recipient of Disneyland’s
complimentary tickets is a local elected official,
such as a city
councilmember,
and the tickets are deemed to be gifts to the local
elected official,
we would remind you that such a city official is
also subject to the gift limits described in Section 89501 above.
Questions
1 and 2:
Are complimentary admission tickets to
Disneyland deemed to be gifts or in-kind contributions to a
recipient official?
This question was asked in a prior Commission
Opinion,
In re Hopkins
(1977)
3 FPPC 107 by the former Anaheim
City Attorney, William Hopkins, also on behalf of the members of
the Anaheim City Council and other city officials.
In Hopkins, the city attorney asked if items such as
complimentary tickets and free annual passes to, for example,
tourist attractions such as Disneyland, must be reported as gifts
on the officials’ Statements of Economic Interests.
The
Commission answered in the affirmative:
Section 82028 defines ‘gift’ to mean ‘any
payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received....’
‘Payment,’
in turn, is defined in Section 82044 to mean ‘a
payment, distribution, transfer,
loan advance,
deposit, gift or other rendering of money,
property, services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible.’
(Emphasis added.)
Since the complimentary tickets and free passes


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page 7
entitled the holder to attend specified events
without paying the admission price charged to other
members of the public,
such tickets constitute
items of value.
Thus, the tickets are ‘payments’
within the meaning of Section 82044.
Since they
are complimentary, we assume that members of the
council have not pro7ided equal or greater
consideration in return for them.
Accordingly,
a
complimentary ticket is a gift which must be
reported by the recipient on his or her Statement
of Economic Interests
Our conclusion is bolstered by Section 82030
which specifically provides that income includes
any ‘discount in the price of anything of value
unless the discount is available to members of the
public without regard to official status.’
In the
instant case, the free passes and complimentary
tickets provide a discount of the entire purchase
price and are made available specifically because
of the recipient’s official status.
Hopkins, sura at 110—111.
Numerous letters have been submitted over the years
concerning tickets to various events and in almost all instances,
tickets have been treated as gifts.
3
Furthermore, the treatment
of tickets as gifts has been codified.
See Regulation 18726,
Regulation 18726.3
(valuation of gift passes and season tickets),
Regulation 18726.7
(passes or tickets given to an agency),
and
Regulation 18726.8
(valuation of gift tickets to charitable and
political fundraisers).
Although it may be theoretically possible
that under some set of circumstances, providing a ticket to a
public official may constitute an in-kind contribution,
such is
not the case in the Disneyland situation you describe.
4
Concluding that complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
are deemed to be gifts under the Act,
we then examine the next
issue:
who is the recipient of the gift in those situations where
the tickets are provided directly by Disneyland to third parties
at the suggestion or recommendation of a member of the Anaheim
City Council or the Anaheim City Manager?
Is the donee the
Anaheim official who makes the suggestion or recommendation that
Disneyland issue the complimentary tickets to a third party?
Or,
is the donee the third party who actually picks up the tickets at
the “Will Call” window?
Sometimes free tickets and passes have been categorized as
compensation.
(Lamoree Advice Letter,
No. A-86-034.)
See Adkisson Advice Letter,
No. A-91-512 for a situation where
payment was considered a contribution and not a gift.


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page
8
The answer to these questions rests in large part on the
Commission’s interpretation of Regulation 18726.1 which provides:
A gift is “received by” an official when it is
delivered to the official and is not disposed of
pursuant to subsection (b).
If a gift is only
offered, but the official rejects the offer or
declines to accept the gift,
no gift has been
received.
A gift is “provided to, received by, or
promised to” an official at the time the offer is
extended if the gift is offered to the official in
such a manner that it is within the official’s
discretion whether or not to accept it and the
official ultimately decides to accept the gift.
A
gift is deemed accepted if it is tendered and comes
under the control of the recipient official and he
or she neither returns it, donates it, nor
reimburses the donor for it as provided in
subsection (b).
Discarding or otherwise turning a
gift over to another person (other than as provided
in subsection
(b))
constitutes acceptanóe of the
gift by the official.
5
(Emphasis added.)
Regulation 18726.1 provides that if.the official does not
return the ticket, donate it to charity or to a public agency, or
reimburse the donor within thirty days, the ticket must be
disclosed.
In addition, the regulation codifies long-standing
Commission advice that a gift of a free ticket is received by an
official for disqualification purposes when he or she exercises
discretion and control over the ticket.
In your letter,
you take the position that because the Walt
Disney Company is the ultimate arbiter of whether it will honor,
modify, or reject the public official’s request that complimentary
admission tickets be given to a specified third party, the Anaheim
public official does not have discretion and control over the
Disneyland tickets.
Furthermore, you suggest that because tickets
are held at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window in the name of the
specified third party and only that party may pick up the tickets,
this factor also argues in support of your conclusion.
We do not
agree that these are the sole factors to consider.
It has been long-standing Commission advice that where the
tickets and passes are intended for distribution to others at the
public official’s discretion, the tickets and passes are deemed to
The Commission is currently analyzing this regulation to
determine whether it should be modified or clarified.
Please
note, therefore, that if the regulation is significantly modified,
this advice could change.
(See Proposed Regulations 18941 and
18943.)


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page
9
be a gift to the public official.
(Lamoree Advice Letter, suDra.)
Although most of our previous advice involved situations in which
the official had actual physical possession of the complimentary
tickets over which the official had discretionary use
(Robbins
Advice Letter,
No. A-77-392; Delventhal Advice Letter,
No. 1-86-
019), there have been at least two instances in which the
Commission found discretion and control based on constructive
possession of the free tickets.
One instance is the Urch Advice Letter,
No. A-91-391 which
does not apply to your situation because the fact situation is not
substantially similar.
6
A second instance occurred in 1977 at an
opinion request meeting wherein commission staff confronted the
identical issue raised in your letter.
(M-77—493.)
The issue was
whether a legislator had received a gift from a racetrack when he
asked the track to provide free passes to four of his
constituents.
Commission staff determined under the facts
provided that the legislator received a gift when he exercised
discretion and control over the complimentary tickets.
7
Your letter makes it clear that it is the custom and practice
of Disney to honor requests and recommendations of Anaheim public
officials as well as those of other public officials, i.e. the
Governor’s office, for complimentary admission tickets to
Disneyland.
You acknowledge that Disneyland internally credits
the requesting public official with the complimentary tickets and
provides such officials with information for their Statements of
Economic Interests.
The statement provided by Disney contains the
value of all regular admittance tickets given to such officials
for their personal or discretionary use and the number of tickets
that were left for third parties at the “Will Call” window in
response to the officials’ requests for same.
Under the facts presented, it appears that Disney has placed
complimentary tickets to Disneyland at the disposal of Anaheim
public officials and other public officials.
Anaheim city
6
In Urch,
we were informed that Disneyland’s ticket request
policy did not “carry over” to elected officials other than those
representing the City of Anaheim.
Therefore,
in order to secure
complimentary tickets for themselves or their guests, other public
officials in the state had to request those tickets from,
among
others, Assemblymember Umberg.
Under those circumstances we found
that it was Assemblymember Umberg, not the Walt Disney Company,
who was exercising discretion and control over who would actually
use the Disneyland tickets.
Thus, the complimentary admission
tickets were deemed to be gifts to Assemblyiuember Umberg,
and not
to the other public officials for whom he was securing the free
tickets.
Please note that although we concur in the conclusion of that
memorandum, the analysis contained therein is superseded by this
letter.


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page 10
officials frequently ask that complimentary tickets to Disneyland
be provided by Disney for third parties
(as well as themselves)
and Disney honors such requests and recommendations.
It also
appears that some, though not all, of the requests and
recommendations for complimentary tickets for third parties are
made by äity officials in their official capacity and are for
state and federal officers, visiting dignitaries, schools,
and
charitable organizations.
From these facts we conclude that complimentary admission
tickets to Disneyland which are provided by the Walt Disney
Company to third parties such as family members, friends and
constituents at the suggestion, recommendation, or request of
members of the Anaheim City Council or the Anaheim City Manager
are deemed to be gifts from the Walt Disney Company to those city
officials for purposes of the gift limits as well as for the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
8
There is a significant exception to this rule.
Where free
tickets to Disneyland are provided to individuals such as other
public officials or visiting dignitaries, or to groups such as
schools or charitable organizations, at the recommendation or
request of members of the Anaheim City Council or Anaheim City
Manager acting in their official capacity, the tickets will not be
treated as gifts to those city officials.
We will refer to this
factual situation as the “protocol exception.”
Please note that this exception will not apply to Anaheim
city officials or their agents who gain actual physical possession
or have discretion and control over the distribution of the
complimentary tickets to Disneyland.
In that situation, the free
tickets provided by the Anaheim city officials to other public
officials and visiting dignitaries, for example, will also be
treated as gifts to those city officials.
(See Urch Advice
Letter,
No.
A—91-391 referenced in footnote 6.)
In order to avoid the problem of being a recipient of a gift
in the circumstances described here,
we recommend that the city
official refer the third party to the tourist attraction and that
the third party make his or her own arrangements for complimentary
admission tickets with the attraction directly.
Provided that the
8
In response to a general inquiry of whether a public official’s
mere “suggestion” to a donor would mean that the tickets would be
treated as gifts to the official if the donor in fact provided
tickets to the “suggested” parties,
we have advised that generally
they would not be treated as gifts to the official.
(Davis 1-91-
114 and 1-91-143.)
However,
we cautioned that the conclusion
necessarily must be based on the facts of the particular
situation, i.e. whether the gift came under the discretion or
control of the suggesting official.
To the extent this letter is
inconsistent with the advice contained in the Davis letter, this
letter shall supersede it.


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page 11
official or his or her agent does not make a suggestion,
recommendation or request to the tourist attraction that it
provides complimentary tickets for a third party (subject to the
“protocol exception” noted above),
does not deliver the tickets to
the third party,
and does not get involved in the ticket
arrangements in any way other than the referral, the city official
would not be considered the recipient of those free tickets.
If they have not already done so, Disney and other similarly
situated attractions may wish to obviate much of the problem by
assuming more of the burden in these matters, thereby relieving
the governmental officials of the duties they seem to have assumed
over the years in serving as “go—betweens” in the giving away of
free admission tickets.
We might suggest that Disney and other
attractions create a special complimentary tickets desk to which
the public official could simply refer requestors of free tickets.
It would then be up to Disney,
for example, to sift through the
many requests on its own, thereby altogether removing the public
officials from the process.
Likewise,
we recommend that if the Anaheim City Manager and
his or her clerical staff continue to transmit the requests and
recommendations for all city staff as a convenience to Disney,
they disclose to Disney the name and address of the city official
on whose behalf the city manager’s office is calling and the fact
that the city manager is acting as the intermediary of the gift.
Question 3:
You ask if it would make any difference in our
analysis if the city manager had personal knowledge of,
or
screened, the recommendations made to Disney by his staff.
You indicate in your letter that as a convenience to
Disneyland, the Anaheim City Manager and members of his or her
clerical staff transmit requests and recommendations for
complimentary tickets to Disneyland for all city staff, for
residents and businesses both within and without the City of
Anaheim,
and for public officials throughout the state.
Presumably the clerical staff are merely carrying out their job
duties at the direction,
and under the control,
of the city
manager.
As such, the clerical staff are agents of the city
manager.
Thus, whether the city manager screens or has personal
knowledge of the recommendations made by his or her clerical staff
does not alter our conclusion set forth above in response to
Question 2.
question 4:
You ask if it would make any difference in our
analysis if the other city officials on whose behalf the Anaheim
City Manager and his or her staff transmits requests and
recommendations for complimentary tickets to Disneyland also are
subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the
Act.
It would make a difference if the city manager’s office is
acting as an intermediary for a gift when the city manager or a


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page 12
member of his clerical staff transmit requests or recommendations
for complimentary tickets to Disneyland for other city personnel
and these other city personnel are public officials subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
(Sections
82048 and 82019; Regulation 18700(a).)
Sections 87313 and 87210 enunciate the standards for gifts.
made through intermediaries and others.
For example, Section
87313, which applies to ultimate recipients of gifts who are
Section 87300 filers,
in other words, persons required to file a
Form 730, provides:
No person shall make a gift totaling fifty
dollars
($50)
or more in a calendar month on behalf
of another,
or while acting as the intermediary or
agent of another, without disclosing to the
recipient of the gift both his own full name,
street address,
and business activity, if any,
and
the full name, street address,
and business
activity, if any,
of the actual donor.
The
recipient of the gift shall include in his
Statement of Economic Interests the full name,
street address,
and business activity, if any,
of
the intermediary or agent and the actual donor.
In the situation you describe, where the Anaheim City Manager
or his or her clerical staff are transmitting the requests or
recommendations of other city officials for complimentary
admission tickets that are placed at their disposal by Disney, it
appears that the city manager is acting as an intermediary of a
gift for these other city officials who are, in fact, the actual
recipients of the gifts.
If such is the case, the city manager or
his or her agent has a statutory duty to disclose the city
manager’s intermediary status to the recipient city official.
9
You do not indicate in your letter if the other city
officials’ requests or recommendations are made at the direction
of the city manager or a city councilmember.
If such occurs, the
requesting or recommending city official may be the intermediary
of the gift and the-city manager or city councilmember would be
If Disney does not already do so,
we recommend that its
internal records accurately reflect the transaction wherein the
city manager is the intermediary of a gift for the recipient city
official.
Disney may also want to provide informational
statements to each of the recipient city officials rather than to
just the city manager.


Our File No.
1-92-218
Page 13
the actual recipient of the gift.
1
°
Without sufficient factual
information,
we are not able to reach a definitive conclusion in
this regard.
question 5:
Relying on Commission precedent for authorizing
the return of gifts more than 30 days after the actual receipt of
the gift where issues of fairness were at stake, you ask if
Anaheim public officials who otherwise would be required to
disqualify themselves from decisions concerning or affecting
Disney, would be permitted to reimburse Disneyland, if that is
their desire,
and have such repayments fall within the thirty day
reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1.
Based on the law, the Anaheim City Councilmembers and
nonelected city officials should have known that tickets they
requested for themselves,
members of their families and their
friends were gifts which are reportable on their Statements of
Economic Interests.
What you claim they did not realize was that
their ability to secure free tickets to Disneyland on behalf of
their constituents and other such third parties, excluding public
officials, dignitaries, schools and charitable organizations
previously discussed, meant that those tickets also would be
treated as gifts to them.
Section 82028 defines “gift” to exclude gifts which are not
used and which, within 30 days after receipt, are returned to the
donor or donated to a charitable organization without being
claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes.
Regulation
18726.1(b) (4)
has extended the exclusion to permit the official,
within 30 days of receipt, to reimburse the donor or his or her
agent or intermediary for all or a portion of the gift.
In this
event, the value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the
reimbursement.
On three occasions, the Commission has deviated from the
30—
day rule for receipt and return of gifts and permitted the public
officials to cure the problem of potential disqualification.
You
contend that the instant situation is such an occasion.
We
concur.
In Hopkins, the public officials were permitted to return the
gifts within 30 days after the issuance of the Opinion because the
Commission stated that its interpretation of “legally required
participation” was not one which had heretofore been applied.
However, thereafter (after the issuance of Hopkins) gifts which
were not returned promptly to their donors still could trigger
disqualification and the doctrine of legally required
10
As previously mentioned in the Urch Advice Letter,
No. A-91-
391,
a regulation
(Regulation 18945.3)
is being proposed which
directly concerns the definition of an “intermediary of a gift.”
Prenotice discussion of this proposed regulation is currently
scheduled for the Commission’s May 7,
1992 meeting.


Our File No.
1—92-218
Page 14
participation (Section 87101)
could not be invoked to avoid such a
result.
In the case at hand, the gift receipt and acceptance rule is
not new.
However,
our interpretation of discretion and control to
include constructive possession and our application of this
interpretation to complimentary tickets to a tourist attraction
placed at the disposal of a public official for his discretionary
use may not be widely known.
We also understand that members of
the Anaheim City Council and the Anaheim City Manager would be
deemed to have received gifts in those instances where they
secured tickets for their constituents or other such third parties
and that these gifts would subject them to disqualification,
and
for some, would constitute a violation of the gift limits.
We
also note that it was Disney and the public officials themselves,
through their city attorney,
who brought this matter before us for
resolution.
For these reasons,
we conclude that because of possible
confusion concerning whether tickets left for third parties
(excluding tickets for family members and friends> at Disneyland’s
“Will Call” window at the recommendation of Anaheim public
officials are gifts to those city officials,
we will permit those
city officials to return or reimburse Disney within 30 days of
issuance of this letter for tickets received or distributed by
Anaheim public officials within 12 months preceding the date of
this letter.
If that action is taken,
we will not consider the
tickets to be gifts to the officials.
On the other hand, gifts of
tickets which have been received in the past and which are not
returned or reimbursed to Disney following issuance of this letter
may trigger an official’s disqualification.
For purposes of
disqualification, the same applies, of course, to gifts of tickets
worth $250 or more which are received and accepted in the future.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

Alternatively, copis of these materials and

cost of 10 per page.

sections.question:
1.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by the Walt Disney Company
(“Disney”) to third
parties at the suggestion or recommendation of members of the
Anaheim City Council deemed to be in-kind campaign contributions
, et sec.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
1—92-218
or gifts by Disney to those city councilmembers for purposes of
the Act?
2.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by Disney to third parties at the suggestion or
recommendation of the Anaheim City Manager or his or her clerical
staff deemed gifts by Disney to the Anaheim City Manager for
purposes of the Act?
3.
Does the answer to number
2 above depend upon whether the
Anaheim City Manager has personal knowledge of, or screens,
such
suggestions or recommendations?
4.
Is the answer to number
2 above different if the public
official or employee of the City of Anaheim,
who is the recipient
of the complimentary tickets,
is subject to the disclosure and
disqualification requirements of the Act?
5.
If the answers to numbers
1 and
2 above are in the
affirmative and the Commission treats the complimentary admission
tickets to Disneyland as gifts to the requesting or recommending
public officials,
may the affected Anaheim public officials be
permitted to cure the problem of potential disqualification and of
violation of the gift limits by reimbursing Disneyland, if that is
their desire,
and have such repayments fall within the thirty-day
reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1?
sections.conclusion:
1 and 2.
Under the facts provided,
we conclude that
complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland which are provided
by the Walt Disney Company to third parties such as family
members,
friends and constituents at the suggestion,
recommendation, or request of members of the Anaheim City Council
or the Anaheim City Manager are deemed to be gifts from the Walt
Disney Company to those city officials for purposes of the gift
limits as well as for the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act.
However,
a “protocol exception” exists where free tickets to
Disneyland are provided to individuals such as other public
officials or visiting dignitaries, or to groups such as schools or
charitable organizations, at the recommendation or request of
members of the Anaheim City Council or Anaheim City Manager acting
in their official capacity.
In that situation, the tickets will
not be treated as gifts to the city councilmembers or the city
manager.
Please note that the “protocol exception” will not apply to
Anaheim city officials or their agents who gain actual physical
possession or have discretion and control over the distribution of
the complimentary tickets to Disneyland.
In such a case, the free
tickets provided by Anaheim city officials to other public
officials and visiting dignitaries, for example, will also be


Our File No.
1—92—218
treated as gifts to those city officials.
(See Urch Advice
Letter,
No.
A-91—391 referenced in footnote 6.)
3.
The answer is no.
Presumably the clerical staff are
merely carrying out their job duties at the direction,
and under
the control of the city manager.
As such, the clerical staff are
agents of the city manager.
Thus, whether the city manager
screens, or has personal knowledge of, the recommendations made by
his or her clerical staff does not alter our conclusion set forth
above in response to Questions
1 and 2.
4.
The answer is yes.
It would make a difference if the
city manager’s office is acting as an intermediary for a gift when
the city manager or a member of his clerical staff transmits
requests or recommendations for complimentary tickets to
Disneyland for other city personnel
these other city personnel
are public officials subject to the disclosure and,
disqualification provisions of the Act.
5.
Because of possible confusion concerning whether tickets
left for third parties
(excluding tickets for family members and
friends) at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window at the recommendation
of Anaheim public officials are gifts to those city officials,
we
will permit those city officials to return the tickets or
reimburse Disney for the value of the tickets within 30 days of
issuance of this letter (for tickets received or distributed by
Anaheim public officials within 12 months preceding the date of
this letter).
If that action is taken,
we will not consider the
tickets to be gifts to the officials.
On the other hand, gifts of
tickets which have been received in the past and which are not
returned or reimbursed to Disney following issuance of this letter
may trigger an official’s disqualification.
For purposes of
disqualification, the same applies, of course, to gifts of tickets
worth $250 or more which are received and accepted in the future.
sections.facts:
One of the business entities owned by the Walt’ Disney Company
is Disneyland,
a leading California tourist attraction which is
visited by millions of guests each year.
It is your understanding
that the Walt Disney Company provides over 300,000 free admission
tickets each year to a wide variety of individuals, nonprofit
organizations,
media groups,
and charities.
Occasionally, public officials,
including Anaheim City
Councilmembers, the Anaheim City Manager, the Governor’s office,
and other state and federal officers, either request that Walt
Disney Company provide them with complimentary tickets for their
own use or the use of their friends, or recommend that Walt Disney
Company provide third parties with complimentary tickets to
Disneyland.
In your letter you indicate that these third parties
include charities, VIPs,
community groups, constituents,
and other
individuals.


Our File No.
1—92-218
Apparently public officials are not the only persons who
request complimentary tickets for themselves and their- friends or
who recommend that complimentary tickets be provided to third
parties.
You also state in your letter that other persons or
categories of persons who request complimentary tickets for
themselves or for third parties include media executives, media
personalities, entertainment executives, entertainment
personalities, charitable organizations,
community organizations,
sports teams,
and school leaders.
You inform us that the Walt Disney Company itself determines
whether it will honor, modify, or reject the recommendation of a
public official or other requestor for complimentary tickets for
third parties.
If Disney elects to honor an official’s
recommendation and provide free admission tickets to the
recommended group or individual, it leaves the tickets at its
“Will Call” window in the name of that group or individual.
The
recommending party does not determine whether the free tickets
will be issued to the third party,
does not handle the tickets,
and could not themselves pick up the tickets at the “Will Call”
window since the tickets are held in the name of the third party
recipient.
The Anaheim City Manager and his staff also make
recommendations to the Walt Disney Company that it provide
complimentary admission tickets to third parties.
These third
parties include residents and businesses within the City of
Anaheim, residents and businesses in other communities,
and public
officials from other communities.
In addition, as a convenience to the Walt Disney Company, the
clerical staff in the Anaheim City Manager’s office transmits
requests or recommendations for complimentary tickets for all the
nonelected public officials and employees of the City of Anaheim.
You state that the Anaheim City Manager does not personally review
or approve such requests except in unusual circumstances.
Many of
the city’s nonelected public officials and employees who request
complimentary tickets through the Anaheim City Manager’s office
are also subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Act.
Disney currently records such requests
as coming from the Anaheim City Manager and not from the recipient
or recommending Anaheim public officials or employees.
After the end of each calendar year, Disneyland provides to
the Anaheim City Councilmembers and to the Anaheim City-Manager a
statement reflecting the value of each Disneyland special event
attended by such public officials and the value of all regular
complimentary admission tickets provided to such officials for
their personal or discretionary use.
Disneyland sends this
information to assist the city’s public officials in preparing
“Schedule F” of their respective annual Statements of Economic
Interests.
Recently, Disneyland additionally began including on
this statement the number of complimentary tickets that were left
for third parties at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window in response


Our File No.
1—92-218
to recommendations received from the public official or the public
official’s office.
sections.analysis:
Pursuant to the Act,
every public official must disclose all
his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected
by the exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200—87313.)
A “public official” is defined broadly to include
every natural person who is a member, officer,
employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.)
Section 87207 provides that a public official
must disclose the name and address of each source of gifts of $50
or more in value, the amount and the date on which the gift was
received,
and a general description of the business activity of
the donor.
Moreover, Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making or in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial
interest.
(Section 87100.)
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on any donor of,
or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
$250 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).)
And finally, the Act now imposes gift and honoraria limits
with respect to local elected officers.
Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal,
or like
gathering, or any gifts,
from any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
Section 82028(a) defines a “gift” as:
[A]ny payment to the extent that consideration
of eaual or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.


Our Page
6
Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal
action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by
reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
of proving that the consideration received is of
equal or greater value.
(Emphasis added.)
You have asked a series of general questions concerning the
characterization of free admission tickets to Disneyland,
and if
the tickets are deemed to be gifts, the identity of the donee
under various circumstances.
At the outset of our analysis,
we
would like to clarify two significant points.
First, your letter distinguishes between elected and
nonelected public officials.
The Act makes no such distinction
for financial disclosure or disqualification purposes.
The
definition of “public official” includes elected and nonelected
officials.
The term public official includes every member,
officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency.
(Section 82048.)
Thus, the issue of whether or not the
Disneyland tickets discussed herein are gifts does not turn on the
elected or nonelected status of the concerned public officials.
Second, all the questions addressed in your letter concern
application of the Act’s disclosure and disqualification
provisions.
If,
however, the recipient of Disneyland’s
complimentary tickets is a local elected official,
such as a city
councilmember,
and the tickets are deemed to be gifts to the local
elected official,
we would remind you that such a city official is
also subject to the gift limits described in Section 89501 above.
Questions
1 and 2:
Are complimentary admission tickets to
Disneyland deemed to be gifts or in-kind contributions to a
recipient official?
This question was asked in a prior Commission
Opinion,
In re Hopkins
(1977)
3 FPPC 107 by the former Anaheim
City Attorney, William Hopkins, also on behalf of the members of
the Anaheim City Council and other city officials.
In Hopkins, the city attorney asked if items such as
complimentary tickets and free annual passes to, for example,
tourist attractions such as Disneyland, must be reported as gifts
on the officials’ Statements of Economic Interests.
The
Commission answered in the affirmative:
Section 82028 defines ‘gift’ to mean ‘any
payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received....’
‘Payment,’
in turn, is defined in Section 82044 to mean ‘a
payment, distribution, transfer,
loan advance,
deposit, gift or other rendering of money,
property, services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible.’
(Emphasis added.)
Since the complimentary tickets and free passes


Our File No.
1—92-218
entitled the holder to attend specified events
without paying the admission price charged to other
members of the public,
such tickets constitute
items of value.
Thus, the tickets are ‘payments’
within the meaning of Section 82044.
Since they
are complimentary, we assume that members of the
council have not pro7ided equal or greater
consideration in return for them.
Accordingly,
a
complimentary ticket is a gift which must be
reported by the recipient on his or her Statement
of Economic Interests
Our conclusion is bolstered by Section 82030
which specifically provides that income includes
any ‘discount in the price of anything of value
unless the discount is available to members of the
public without regard to official status.’
In the
instant case, the free passes and complimentary
tickets provide a discount of the entire purchase
price and are made available specifically because
of the recipient’s official status.
Hopkins, sura at 110—111.
Numerous letters have been submitted over the years
concerning tickets to various events and in almost all instances,
tickets have been treated as gifts.
3
Furthermore, the treatment
of tickets as gifts has been codified.
See Regulation 18726,
Regulation 18726.3
(valuation of gift passes and season tickets),
Regulation 18726.7
(passes or tickets given to an agency),
and
Regulation 18726.8
(valuation of gift tickets to charitable and
political fundraisers).
Although it may be theoretically possible
that under some set of circumstances, providing a ticket to a
public official may constitute an in-kind contribution,
such is
not the case in the Disneyland situation you describe.
4
Concluding that complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
are deemed to be gifts under the Act,
we then examine the next
issue:
who is the recipient of the gift in those situations where
the tickets are provided directly by Disneyland to third parties
at the suggestion or recommendation of a member of the Anaheim
City Council or the Anaheim City Manager?
Is the donee the
Anaheim official who makes the suggestion or recommendation that
Disneyland issue the complimentary tickets to a third party?
Or,
is the donee the third party who actually picks up the tickets at
the “Will Call” window?
Sometimes free tickets and passes have been categorized as
compensation.
(Lamoree Advice Letter,
No. A-86-034.)
See Adkisson Advice Letter,
No. A-91-512 for a situation where
payment was considered a contribution and not a gift.


Our Page
8
The answer to these questions rests in large part on the
Commission’s interpretation of Regulation 18726.1 which provides:
A gift is “received by” an official when it is
delivered to the official and is not disposed of
pursuant to subsection (b).
If a gift is only
offered, but the official rejects the offer or
declines to accept the gift,
no gift has been
received.
A gift is “provided to, received by, or
promised to” an official at the time the offer is
extended if the gift is offered to the official in
such a manner that it is within the official’s
discretion whether or not to accept it and the
official ultimately decides to accept the gift.
A
gift is deemed accepted if it is tendered and comes
under the control of the recipient official and he
or she neither returns it, donates it, nor
reimburses the donor for it as provided in
subsection (b).
Discarding or otherwise turning a
gift over to another person (other than as provided
in subsection
(b))
constitutes acceptanóe of the
gift by the official.
5
(Emphasis added.)
Regulation 18726.1 provides that if.the official does not
return the ticket, donate it to charity or to a public agency, or
reimburse the donor within thirty days, the ticket must be
disclosed.
In addition, the regulation codifies long-standing
Commission advice that a gift of a free ticket is received by an
official for disqualification purposes when he or she exercises
discretion and control over the ticket.
In your letter,
you take the position that because the Walt
Disney Company is the ultimate arbiter of whether it will honor,
modify, or reject the public official’s request that complimentary
admission tickets be given to a specified third party, the Anaheim
public official does not have discretion and control over the
Disneyland tickets.
Furthermore, you suggest that because tickets
are held at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window in the name of the
specified third party and only that party may pick up the tickets,
this factor also argues in support of your conclusion.
We do not
agree that these are the sole factors to consider.
It has been long-standing Commission advice that where the
tickets and passes are intended for distribution to others at the
public official’s discretion, the tickets and passes are deemed to
The Commission is currently analyzing this regulation to
determine whether it should be modified or clarified.
Please
note, therefore, that if the regulation is significantly modified,
this advice could change.
(See Proposed Regulations 18941 and
18943.)


Our Page
9
be a gift to the public official.
(Lamoree Advice Letter, suDra.)
Although most of our previous advice involved situations in which
the official had actual physical possession of the complimentary
tickets over which the official had discretionary use
(Robbins
Advice Letter,
No. A-77-392; Delventhal Advice Letter,
No. 1-86-
019), there have been at least two instances in which the
Commission found discretion and control based on constructive
possession of the free tickets.
One instance is the Urch Advice Letter,
No. A-91-391 which
does not apply to your situation because the fact situation is not
substantially similar.
6
A second instance occurred in 1977 at an
opinion request meeting wherein commission staff confronted the
identical issue raised in your letter.
(M-77—493.)
The issue was
whether a legislator had received a gift from a racetrack when he
asked the track to provide free passes to four of his
constituents.
Commission staff determined under the facts
provided that the legislator received a gift when he exercised
discretion and control over the complimentary tickets.
7
Your letter makes it clear that it is the custom and practice
of Disney to honor requests and recommendations of Anaheim public
officials as well as those of other public officials, i.e. the
Governor’s office, for complimentary admission tickets to
Disneyland.
You acknowledge that Disneyland internally credits
the requesting public official with the complimentary tickets and
provides such officials with information for their Statements of
Economic Interests.
The statement provided by Disney contains the
value of all regular admittance tickets given to such officials
for their personal or discretionary use and the number of tickets
that were left for third parties at the “Will Call” window in
response to the officials’ requests for same.
Under the facts presented, it appears that Disney has placed
complimentary tickets to Disneyland at the disposal of Anaheim
public officials and other public officials.
Anaheim city
6
In Urch,
we were informed that Disneyland’s ticket request
policy did not “carry over” to elected officials other than those
representing the City of Anaheim.
Therefore,
in order to secure
complimentary tickets for themselves or their guests, other public
officials in the state had to request those tickets from,
among
others, Assemblymember Umberg.
Under those circumstances we found
that it was Assemblymember Umberg, not the Walt Disney Company,
who was exercising discretion and control over who would actually
use the Disneyland tickets.
Thus, the complimentary admission
tickets were deemed to be gifts to Assemblyiuember Umberg,
and not
to the other public officials for whom he was securing the free
tickets.
Please note that although we concur in the conclusion of that
memorandum, the analysis contained therein is superseded by this
letter.


Our Page 10
officials frequently ask that complimentary tickets to Disneyland
be provided by Disney for third parties
(as well as themselves)
and Disney honors such requests and recommendations.
It also
appears that some, though not all, of the requests and
recommendations for complimentary tickets for third parties are
made by äity officials in their official capacity and are for
state and federal officers, visiting dignitaries, schools,
and
charitable organizations.
From these facts we conclude that complimentary admission
tickets to Disneyland which are provided by the Walt Disney
Company to third parties such as family members, friends and
constituents at the suggestion, recommendation, or request of
members of the Anaheim City Council or the Anaheim City Manager
are deemed to be gifts from the Walt Disney Company to those city
officials for purposes of the gift limits as well as for the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
8
There is a significant exception to this rule.
Where free
tickets to Disneyland are provided to individuals such as other
public officials or visiting dignitaries, or to groups such as
schools or charitable organizations, at the recommendation or
request of members of the Anaheim City Council or Anaheim City
Manager acting in their official capacity, the tickets will not be
treated as gifts to those city officials.
We will refer to this
factual situation as the “protocol exception.”
Please note that this exception will not apply to Anaheim
city officials or their agents who gain actual physical possession
or have discretion and control over the distribution of the
complimentary tickets to Disneyland.
In that situation, the free
tickets provided by the Anaheim city officials to other public
officials and visiting dignitaries, for example, will also be
treated as gifts to those city officials.
(See Urch Advice
Letter,
No.
A—91-391 referenced in footnote 6.)
In order to avoid the problem of being a recipient of a gift
in the circumstances described here,
we recommend that the city
official refer the third party to the tourist attraction and that
the third party make his or her own arrangements for complimentary
admission tickets with the attraction directly.
Provided that the
8
In response to a general inquiry of whether a public official’s
mere “suggestion” to a donor would mean that the tickets would be
treated as gifts to the official if the donor in fact provided
tickets to the “suggested” parties,
we have advised that generally
they would not be treated as gifts to the official.
(Davis 1-91-
114 and 1-91-143.)
However,
we cautioned that the conclusion
necessarily must be based on the facts of the particular
situation, i.e. whether the gift came under the discretion or
control of the suggesting official.
To the extent this letter is
inconsistent with the advice contained in the Davis letter, this
letter shall supersede it.


Our Page 11
official or his or her agent does not make a suggestion,
recommendation or request to the tourist attraction that it
provides complimentary tickets for a third party (subject to the
“protocol exception” noted above),
does not deliver the tickets to
the third party,
and does not get involved in the ticket
arrangements in any way other than the referral, the city official
would not be considered the recipient of those free tickets.
If they have not already done so, Disney and other similarly
situated attractions may wish to obviate much of the problem by
assuming more of the burden in these matters, thereby relieving
the governmental officials of the duties they seem to have assumed
over the years in serving as “go—betweens” in the giving away of
free admission tickets.
We might suggest that Disney and other
attractions create a special complimentary tickets desk to which
the public official could simply refer requestors of free tickets.
It would then be up to Disney,
for example, to sift through the
many requests on its own, thereby altogether removing the public
officials from the process.
Likewise,
we recommend that if the Anaheim City Manager and
his or her clerical staff continue to transmit the requests and
recommendations for all city staff as a convenience to Disney,
they disclose to Disney the name and address of the city official
on whose behalf the city manager’s office is calling and the fact
that the city manager is acting as the intermediary of the gift.
Question 3:
You ask if it would make any difference in our
analysis if the city manager had personal knowledge of,
or
screened, the recommendations made to Disney by his staff.
You indicate in your letter that as a convenience to
Disneyland, the Anaheim City Manager and members of his or her
clerical staff transmit requests and recommendations for
complimentary tickets to Disneyland for all city staff, for
residents and businesses both within and without the City of
Anaheim,
and for public officials throughout the state.
Presumably the clerical staff are merely carrying out their job
duties at the direction,
and under the control,
of the city
manager.
As such, the clerical staff are agents of the city
manager.
Thus, whether the city manager screens or has personal
knowledge of the recommendations made by his or her clerical staff
does not alter our conclusion set forth above in response to
Question 2.
question 4:
You ask if it would make any difference in our
analysis if the other city officials on whose behalf the Anaheim
City Manager and his or her staff transmits requests and
recommendations for complimentary tickets to Disneyland also are
subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the
Act.
It would make a difference if the city manager’s office is
acting as an intermediary for a gift when the city manager or a


Our File No.
1—92-218
member of his clerical staff transmit requests or recommendations
for complimentary tickets to Disneyland for other city personnel
and these other city personnel are public officials subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
(Sections
82048 and 82019; Regulation 18700(a).)
Sections 87313 and 87210 enunciate the standards for gifts.
made through intermediaries and others.
For example, Section
87313, which applies to ultimate recipients of gifts who are
Section 87300 filers,
in other words, persons required to file a
Form 730, provides:
No person shall make a gift totaling fifty
dollars
($50)
or more in a calendar month on behalf
of another,
or while acting as the intermediary or
agent of another, without disclosing to the
recipient of the gift both his own full name,
street address,
and business activity, if any,
and
the full name, street address,
and business
activity, if any,
of the actual donor.
The
recipient of the gift shall include in his
Statement of Economic Interests the full name,
street address,
and business activity, if any,
of
the intermediary or agent and the actual donor.
In the situation you describe, where the Anaheim City Manager
or his or her clerical staff are transmitting the requests or
recommendations of other city officials for complimentary
admission tickets that are placed at their disposal by Disney, it
appears that the city manager is acting as an intermediary of a
gift for these other city officials who are, in fact, the actual
recipients of the gifts.
If such is the case, the city manager or
his or her agent has a statutory duty to disclose the city
manager’s intermediary status to the recipient city official.
9
You do not indicate in your letter if the other city
officials’ requests or recommendations are made at the direction
of the city manager or a city councilmember.
If such occurs, the
requesting or recommending city official may be the intermediary
of the gift and the-city manager or city councilmember would be
If Disney does not already do so,
we recommend that its
internal records accurately reflect the transaction wherein the
city manager is the intermediary of a gift for the recipient city
official.
Disney may also want to provide informational
statements to each of the recipient city officials rather than to
just the city manager.


Our Page 13
the actual recipient of the gift.
1
°
Without sufficient factual
information,
we are not able to reach a definitive conclusion in
this regard.
question 5:
Relying on Commission precedent for authorizing
the return of gifts more than 30 days after the actual receipt of
the gift where issues of fairness were at stake, you ask if
Anaheim public officials who otherwise would be required to
disqualify themselves from decisions concerning or affecting
Disney, would be permitted to reimburse Disneyland, if that is
their desire,
and have such repayments fall within the thirty day
reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1.
Based on the law, the Anaheim City Councilmembers and
nonelected city officials should have known that tickets they
requested for themselves,
members of their families and their
friends were gifts which are reportable on their Statements of
Economic Interests.
What you claim they did not realize was that
their ability to secure free tickets to Disneyland on behalf of
their constituents and other such third parties, excluding public
officials, dignitaries, schools and charitable organizations
previously discussed, meant that those tickets also would be
treated as gifts to them.
Section 82028 defines “gift” to exclude gifts which are not
used and which, within 30 days after receipt, are returned to the
donor or donated to a charitable organization without being
claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes.
Regulation
18726.1(b) (4)
has extended the exclusion to permit the official,
within 30 days of receipt, to reimburse the donor or his or her
agent or intermediary for all or a portion of the gift.
In this
event, the value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the
reimbursement.
On three occasions, the Commission has deviated from the
30—
day rule for receipt and return of gifts and permitted the public
officials to cure the problem of potential disqualification.
You
contend that the instant situation is such an occasion.
We
concur.
In Hopkins, the public officials were permitted to return the
gifts within 30 days after the issuance of the Opinion because the
Commission stated that its interpretation of “legally required
participation” was not one which had heretofore been applied.
However, thereafter (after the issuance of Hopkins) gifts which
were not returned promptly to their donors still could trigger
disqualification and the doctrine of legally required
10
As previously mentioned in the Urch Advice Letter,
No. A-91-
391,
a regulation
(Regulation 18945.3)
is being proposed which
directly concerns the definition of an “intermediary of a gift.”
Prenotice discussion of this proposed regulation is currently
scheduled for the Commission’s May 7,
1992 meeting.


Our File No.
1—92-218
participation (Section 87101)
could not be invoked to avoid such a
result.
In the case at hand, the gift receipt and acceptance rule is
not new.
However,
our interpretation of discretion and control to
include constructive possession and our application of this
interpretation to complimentary tickets to a tourist attraction
placed at the disposal of a public official for his discretionary
use may not be widely known.
We also understand that members of
the Anaheim City Council and the Anaheim City Manager would be
deemed to have received gifts in those instances where they
secured tickets for their constituents or other such third parties
and that these gifts would subject them to disqualification,
and
for some, would constitute a violation of the gift limits.
We
also note that it was Disney and the public officials themselves,
through their city attorney,
who brought this matter before us for
resolution.
For these reasons,
we conclude that because of possible
confusion concerning whether tickets left for third parties
(excluding tickets for family members and friends> at Disneyland’s
“Will Call” window at the recommendation of Anaheim public
officials are gifts to those city officials,
we will permit those
city officials to return or reimburse Disney within 30 days of
issuance of this letter for tickets received or distributed by
Anaheim public officials within 12 months preceding the date of
this letter.
If that action is taken,
we will not consider the
tickets to be gifts to the officials.
On the other hand, gifts of
tickets which have been received in the past and which are not
returned or reimbursed to Disney following issuance of this letter
may trigger an official’s disqualification.
For purposes of
disqualification, the same applies, of course, to gifts of tickets
worth $250 or more which are received and accepted in the future.

QUESTION: 1.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by the Walt Disney Company
(“Disney”) to third
parties at the suggestion or recommendation of members of the
Anaheim City Council deemed to be in-kind campaign contributions
, et sec.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
1—92-218
or gifts by Disney to those city councilmembers for purposes of
the Act?
2.
Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland
provided directly by Disney to third parties at the suggestion or
recommendation of the Anaheim City Manager or his or her clerical
staff deemed gifts by Disney to the Anaheim City Manager for
purposes of the Act?
3.
Does the answer to number
2 above depend upon whether the
Anaheim City Manager has personal knowledge of, or screens,
such
suggestions or recommendations?
4.
Is the answer to number
2 above different if the public
official or employee of the City of Anaheim,
who is the recipient
of the complimentary tickets,
is subject to the disclosure and
disqualification requirements of the Act?
5.
If the answers to numbers
1 and
2 above are in the
affirmative and the Commission treats the complimentary admission
tickets to Disneyland as gifts to the requesting or recommending
public officials,
may the affected Anaheim public officials be
permitted to cure the problem of potential disqualification and of
violation of the gift limits by reimbursing Disneyland, if that is
their desire,
and have such repayments fall within the thirty-day
reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1?

CONCLUSION: 1 and 2.
Under the facts provided,
we conclude that
complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland which are provided
by the Walt Disney Company to third parties such as family
members,
friends and constituents at the suggestion,
recommendation, or request of members of the Anaheim City Council
or the Anaheim City Manager are deemed to be gifts from the Walt
Disney Company to those city officials for purposes of the gift
limits as well as for the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act.
However,
a “protocol exception” exists where free tickets to
Disneyland are provided to individuals such as other public
officials or visiting dignitaries, or to groups such as schools or
charitable organizations, at the recommendation or request of
members of the Anaheim City Council or Anaheim City Manager acting
in their official capacity.
In that situation, the tickets will
not be treated as gifts to the city councilmembers or the city
manager.
Please note that the “protocol exception” will not apply to
Anaheim city officials or their agents who gain actual physical
possession or have discretion and control over the distribution of
the complimentary tickets to Disneyland.
In such a case, the free
tickets provided by Anaheim city officials to other public
officials and visiting dignitaries, for example, will also be


Our File No.
1—92—218
treated as gifts to those city officials.
(See Urch Advice
Letter,
No.
A-91—391 referenced in footnote 6.)
3.
The answer is no.
Presumably the clerical staff are
merely carrying out their job duties at the direction,
and under
the control of the city manager.
As such, the clerical staff are
agents of the city manager.
Thus, whether the city manager
screens, or has personal knowledge of, the recommendations made by
his or her clerical staff does not alter our conclusion set forth
above in response to Questions
1 and 2.
4.
The answer is yes.
It would make a difference if the
city manager’s office is acting as an intermediary for a gift when
the city manager or a member of his clerical staff transmits
requests or recommendations for complimentary tickets to
Disneyland for other city personnel
these other city personnel
are public officials subject to the disclosure and,
disqualification provisions of the Act.
5.
Because of possible confusion concerning whether tickets
left for third parties
(excluding tickets for family members and
friends) at Disneyland’s “Will Call” window at the recommendation
of Anaheim public officials are gifts to those city officials,
we
will permit those city officials to return the tickets or
reimburse Disney for the value of the tickets within 30 days of
issuance of this letter (for tickets received or distributed by
Anaheim public officials within 12 months preceding the date of
this letter).
If that action is taken,
we will not consider the
tickets to be gifts to the officials.
On the other hand, gifts of
tickets which have been received in the past and which are not
returned or reimbursed to Disney following issuance of this letter
may trigger an official’s disqualification.
For purposes of
disqualification, the same applies, of course, to gifts of tickets
worth $250 or more which are received and accepted in the future.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 29, 1992 Jack White City Attorney City of Anaheim 200 South Anaheim Boulevard Suite 356 Anaheim, CA 92805 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1—92—218 Dear Mr. White: This •is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of public officials of the City of Anaheim, including city counciliuembers, the city manager, and other employees of the city regarding their responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Since you have asked general questions regarding these local public officials, and you have not named a specific elected official on whose behalf you have requested this advice, we provide the following informal responses to your questions. 2 Please be aware that advice letters are based on the particular facts and circumstances of each situation. Thus, these responses to your hypothetical questions may not be applicable to a particular factual situation. QUESTIONS 1. Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland provided directly by the Walt Disney Company (“Disney”) to third parties at the suggestion or recommendation of members of the Anaheim City Council deemed to be in-kind campaign contributions 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. 1—92-218 Page 2 or gifts by Disney to those city councilmembers for purposes of the Act? 2. Are complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland provided directly by Disney to third parties at the suggestion or recommendation of the Anaheim City Manager or his or her clerical staff deemed gifts by Disney to the Anaheim City Manager for purposes of the Act? 3. Does the answer to number 2 above depend upon whether the Anaheim City Manager has personal knowledge of, or screens, such suggestions or recommendations? 4. Is the answer to number 2 above different if the public official or employee of the City of Anaheim, who is the recipient of the complimentary tickets, is subject to the disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Act? 5. If the answers to numbers 1 and 2 above are in the affirmative and the Commission treats the complimentary admission tickets to Disneyland as gifts to the requesting or recommending public officials, may the affected Anaheim public officials be permitted to cure the problem of potential disqualification and of violation of the gift limits by reimbursing Disneyland, if that is their desire, and have such repayments fall within the thirty-day reimbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.1?
